title: Extending mlr3 to Time Series Forecasting
text: mlr3forecast extends mlr3 ecosystem time series forecasting. introduces forecasting task, forecasting learners, temporal resampling strategies, forecasting measures, feature-engineering pipe operators, forecasters behave like mlr3 learner — ready tuning, benchmarking, pipelines, ensembling. glance, mlr3forecast provides: Classical forecasters wrapping forecast, smooth, prophet, tscount — 30 total, baselines (fcst.mean, fcst.random_walk) ARIMA (fcst.auto_arima), ETS, theta, TBATS, prophet, neural nets (fcst.nnetar), count models (fcst.tscount). See mlr_learners$keys("^fcst") full list. Machine learning forecasting turns regr learner forecaster via lag features, recursive (one model applied iteratively) direct (one model per horizon) strategies. Forecasting tasks temporal resamplings (fcst.holdout, fcst.cv) respect order observations, plus global (longitudinal) forecasting across many series. Feature-engineering pipe operators fcst.lags, fcst.rolling, fcst.fourier, fcst.feasts, fcst.tsfeats. Forecasting measures including MASE, RMSSE, Pinball, Winkler, coverage, MSIS (see mlr_measures$keys("^fcst") full list). Full mlr3 integration: tuning mlr3tuning, benchmarking, target transformations, ensembling mlr3pipelines. Example tasks get started — airpassengers, electricity, livestock, lynx, usaccdeaths. List .data.table(mlr_tasks)[task_type == "fcst"]. now forecasting task learner restricted time series regression, may extended classification future. Jump examples : Classical forecasters Machine learning forecasters Benchmarking, ensembling, tuning Global forecasting

title: Extending mlr3 to Time Series Forecasting
text: Native forecasting learners provided packages forecast, smooth, prophet, tscount.  forecast beyond observed data, generate_newdata() builds future rows (missing targets) predict_newdata() fills : forecast() helper combines two steps, generating future rows predicting single call: Target transformations can applied wrapping learner ppl("targettrafo"): predict types learner supports (e.g. "quantiles", "se") listed predict_types: Classical forecasters can return predictive distribution quantiles, scored probabilistic measures Pinball loss: Forecasting resamplings respect temporal order observations:
code: library(mlr3forecast) library(mlr3pipelines) library(ggplot2)  task = tsk("airpassengers") task #>  #> ── <TaskFcst> (144x1): Monthly Airline Passenger Numbers 1949-1960 ───────────── #> • Target: passengers #> • Properties: ordered #> • Order by: month #> • Frequency: month  # or plot the task autoplot(task) # train a forecast learner learner = lrn("fcst.auto_arima")$train(task) prediction = learner$predict(task, 140:144) prediction #>  #> ── <PredictionRegr> for 5 observations: ──────────────────────────────────────── #>  row_ids truth response      month #>      140   606 623.9219 1960-08-01 #>      141   508 513.8585 1960-09-01 #>      142   461 450.7762 1960-10-01 #>      143   390 410.8961 1960-11-01 #>      144   432 439.9462 1960-12-01 prediction$score(msr("regr.rmse")) #> regr.rmse  #>  13.85518 # generate new data to forecast unseen data newdata = generate_newdata(task, 12L) head(newdata) #>         month passengers #> 1: 1961-01-01         NA #> 2: 1961-02-01         NA #> 3: 1961-03-01         NA #> 4: 1961-04-01         NA #> 5: 1961-05-01         NA #> 6: 1961-06-01         NA prediction = learner$predict_newdata(newdata, task) prediction #>  #> ── <PredictionRegr> for 12 observations: ─────────────────────────────────────── #>  row_ids truth response      month #>        1    NA 445.6351 1961-01-01 #>        2    NA 420.3953 1961-02-01 #>        3    NA 449.1988 1961-03-01 #>      ---   ---      ---        --- #>       10    NA 494.1275 1961-10-01 #>       11    NA 423.3336 1961-11-01 #>       12    NA 465.5085 1961-12-01 forecast(learner, task, 12L) #>  #> ── <PredictionRegr> for 12 observations: ─────────────────────────────────────── #>  row_ids truth response      month #>        1    NA 445.6351 1961-01-01 #>        2    NA 420.3953 1961-02-01 #>        3    NA 449.1988 1961-03-01 #>      ---   ---      ---        --- #>       10    NA 494.1275 1961-10-01 #>       11    NA 423.3336 1961-11-01 #>       12    NA 465.5085 1961-12-01 # add a target log transformation learner = as_learner(ppl(   "targettrafo",   graph = lrn("fcst.auto_arima"),   targetmutate.trafo = function(x) log(x),   targetmutate.inverter = function(x) list(response = exp(x$response)) )) prediction = learner$train(task)$predict(task, 140:144) prediction$score(msr("regr.rmse")) #> regr.rmse  #>  12.29896 lrn("fcst.auto_arima")$predict_types #> [1] "response"  "quantiles" # works with quantile response learner = lrn(   "fcst.auto_arima",   predict_type = "quantiles",   quantiles = c(0.1, 0.15, 0.5, 0.85, 0.9),   quantile_response = 0.5 )$train(task, 1:132) learner$predict_newdata(newdata, task) #>  #> ── <PredictionRegr> for 12 observations: ─────────────────────────────────────── #>  row_ids truth     q0.1    q0.15     q0.5    q0.85     q0.9 response      month #>        1    NA 410.6811 413.2496 424.1099 434.9702 437.5387 424.1099 1961-01-01 #>        2    NA 390.2142 393.4355 407.0557 420.6759 423.8972 407.0557 1961-02-01 #>        3    NA 450.7334 454.5764 470.8257 487.0751 490.9181 470.8257 1961-03-01 #>      ---   ---      ---      ---      ---      ---      ---      ---        --- #>       10    NA 436.9438 443.6242 471.8707 500.1173 506.7976 471.8707 1961-10-01 #>       11    NA 390.3115 397.3040 426.8707 456.4374 463.4300 426.8707 1961-11-01 #>       12    NA 431.7490 439.0404 469.8707 500.7011 507.9925 469.8707 1961-12-01 learner$predict(task, 133:144)$score(msr("fcst.pinball")) #> fcst.pinball  #>     11.38111 # resampling, scored with a forecasting measure (MASE) learner = lrn("fcst.auto_arima") resampling = rsmp("fcst.holdout", ratio = 0.7) rr = resample(task, learner, resampling) rr$aggregate(msrs(c("regr.rmse", "fcst.mase"))) #> regr.rmse fcst.mase  #> 27.121096  1.212049

title: Extending mlr3 to Time Series Forecasting
text: regression learner can turned forecaster recursive_forecaster(), adds lag features forecasts recursively:
code: library(mlr3learners)  task = tsk("airpassengers") learner = lrn("regr.ranger") flrn = recursive_forecaster(learner, lags = 1:12)$train(task) newdata = generate_newdata(task, 12L) prediction = flrn$predict_newdata(newdata, task) prediction #>  #> ── <PredictionRegr> for 12 observations: ─────────────────────────────────────── #>  row_ids truth response      month #>        1    NA 446.2509 1961-01-01 #>        2    NA 448.8850 1961-02-01 #>        3    NA 468.0311 1961-03-01 #>      ---   ---      ---        --- #>       10    NA 497.0462 1961-10-01 #>       11    NA 457.9251 1961-11-01 #>       12    NA 458.8529 1961-12-01 prediction = flrn$predict(task, 140:144) prediction #>  #> ── <PredictionRegr> for 5 observations: ──────────────────────────────────────── #>  row_ids truth response      month #>      140   606 564.3768 1960-08-01 #>      141   508 510.6362 1960-09-01 #>      142   461 461.5428 1960-10-01 #>      143   390 418.9087 1960-11-01 #>      144   432 443.4447 1960-12-01 prediction$score(msr("regr.rmse")) #> regr.rmse  #>  23.26557  flrn = recursive_forecaster(learner, lags = 1:12) resampling = rsmp("fcst.holdout", ratio = 0.9) rr = resample(task, flrn, resampling) rr$aggregate(msr("regr.rmse")) #> regr.rmse  #>  51.02657  resampling = rsmp("fcst.cv") rr = resample(task, flrn, resampling) rr$aggregate(msr("regr.rmse")) #> regr.rmse  #>  33.88559

title: Extending mlr3 to Time Series Forecasting
text: recursive_forecaster() builds recursive forecaster (one model, applied iteratively). Use direct_forecaster() horizons train one model per horizon instead — predictions come straight horizon’s model, error accumulation:
code: task = tsk("airpassengers") flrn = direct_forecaster(   lrn("regr.ranger"),   lags = 1:12,   horizons = 12 )$train(task, 1:132) flrn$predict(task, 133:144)$score(msr("regr.rmse")) #> regr.rmse  #>  55.71677

title: Extending mlr3 to Time Series Forecasting
text: Lag features can combined transformations using mlr3pipelines: Use selector_fcst_lags() apply transformations lag features, e.g. log-transforming lags leaving date features untouched:
code: library(mlr3pipelines)  task = tsk("airpassengers") task$set_col_roles("month", add = "feature") graph = po("fcst.lags", lags = 1:12) %>>%   po(     "datefeatures",     param_vals = list(       week_of_year = FALSE,       day_of_year = FALSE,       day_of_month = FALSE,       day_of_week = FALSE     )   ) %>>%   lrn("regr.ranger") flrn = recursive_forecaster(graph)$train(task) prediction = flrn$predict(task, 142:144) prediction$score(msr("regr.rmse")) #> regr.rmse  #>  16.76188 task = tsk("airpassengers") task$set_col_roles("month", add = "feature") graph = po("fcst.lags", lags = 1:12) %>>%   po("colapply", applicator = log, affect_columns = selector_fcst_lags()) %>>%   po(     "datefeatures",     param_vals = list(       week_of_year = FALSE,       day_of_year = FALSE,       day_of_month = FALSE,       day_of_week = FALSE     )   ) %>>%   lrn("regr.ranger") flrn = recursive_forecaster(graph)$train(task) prediction = flrn$predict(task, 142:144) prediction$score(msr("regr.rmse")) #> regr.rmse  #>  13.70027

title: Extending mlr3 to Time Series Forecasting
text: Target transformations can applied wrapping forecast learner ppl("targettrafo"). lags created transformed target predictions automatically inverted back original scale: Ready-made po("fcst.targetboxcox") po("fcst.targetdiff") pipeops also available Box-Cox transformation differencing.
code: task = tsk("airpassengers") graph = po("fcst.lags", lags = 1:12) %>>% lrn("regr.ranger") pipeline = ppl(   "targettrafo",   graph = recursive_forecaster(graph),   targetmutate.trafo = function(x) log(x),   targetmutate.inverter = function(x) list(response = exp(x$response)) ) learner = as_learner(pipeline)$train(task) prediction = learner$predict(task, 142:144) prediction$score(msr("regr.rmse")) #> regr.rmse  #>  15.04127

title: Extending mlr3 to Time Series Forecasting
text: Forecasting tasks can include exogenous covariates. electricity demand forecast lags, calendar features, external regressors (temperature, holiday) supplied forecast horizon:
code: library(mlr3learners) library(mlr3pipelines)  task = tsk("electricity") task$set_col_roles("date", add = "feature") graph = po("fcst.lags", lags = 1:3) %>>%   po("datefeatures", param_vals = list(year = FALSE)) %>>%   lrn("regr.ranger") flrn = recursive_forecaster(graph)$train(task)  max_date = task$data()[.N, date] newdata = data.table(   date = max_date + 1:14,   demand = rep(NA_real_, 14L),   temperature = 26,   holiday = c(TRUE, rep(FALSE, 13L)) ) prediction = flrn$predict_newdata(newdata, task) prediction #>  #> ── <PredictionRegr> for 14 observations: ─────────────────────────────────────── #>  row_ids truth response       date #>        1    NA 187707.3 2015-01-01 #>        2    NA 196950.8 2015-01-02 #>        3    NA 190586.9 2015-01-03 #>      ---   ---      ---        --- #>       12    NA 222740.3 2015-01-12 #>       13    NA 227257.2 2015-01-13 #>       14    NA 228954.2 2015-01-14

title: Extending mlr3 to Time Series Forecasting
text: ML forecasters declare task_type = "fcst", can benchmarked side--side classical learners task single benchmark() call:
code: task = tsk("airpassengers") resampling = rsmp("fcst.holdout", ratio = 0.9)$instantiate(task) n_test = length(resampling$test_set(1L))  learners = list(   lrn("fcst.arima", id = "arima"),   recursive_forecaster(lrn("regr.ranger"), lags = 1:12, id = "ranger_recursive"),   direct_forecaster(     lrn("regr.ranger"),     lags = 1:12,     horizons = n_test,     id = "ranger_direct"   ) ) design = benchmark_grid(task, learners, resampling) bmr = benchmark(design) bmr$aggregate(msr("regr.rmse"))[, .(learner_id, regr.rmse)] #>          learner_id regr.rmse #> 1:            arima 216.31005 #> 2: ranger_recursive  50.70191 #> 3:    ranger_direct  52.02509

title: Extending mlr3 to Time Series Forecasting
text: Forecast learners produce regression predictions hood, standard mlr3pipelines ensemble pattern works directly: branch several forecasters gunion() average forecasts po("regravg"). mirrors idea behind forecastHybrid package, mix classical ML learners.
code: task = tsk("airpassengers")  graph = gunion(list(   po("learner", lrn("fcst.auto_arima"), id = "arima"),   po("learner", lrn("fcst.ets"), id = "ets"),   po("learner", lrn("fcst.theta"), id = "theta") )) %>>%   po("regravg") flrn = as_learner(graph)$train(task) forecast(flrn, task, 12L) #>  #> ── <PredictionRegr> for 12 observations: ─────────────────────────────────────── #>  row_ids truth response #>        1    NA 442.5050 #>        2    NA 427.6327 #>        3    NA 478.5120 #>      ---   ---      --- #>       10    NA 471.2626 #>       11    NA 408.0117 #>       12    NA 455.0409 flrn$predict(task, 140:144)$score(msr("regr.rmse")) #> regr.rmse  #>  12.23143  # weight the members instead of averaging equally graph$param_set$set_values(regravg.weights = c(0.5, 0.3, 0.2)) flrn = as_learner(graph)$train(task) flrn$predict(task, 140:144)$score(msr("regr.rmse")) #> regr.rmse  #>  12.28049

title: Extending mlr3 to Time Series Forecasting
text: Forecast learners regular mlr3 learners, plug standard mlr3tuning machinery. Mark hyperparameters to_tune() wrap learner auto_tuner(), using forecasting resampling fcst.holdout fcst.cv respect temporal order: Classical forecasters tune way:
code: library(mlr3tuning)  task = tsk("airpassengers")  # tune an ML forecaster flrn = recursive_forecaster(lrn("regr.ranger"), lags = 1:12) flrn$param_set$set_values(   regr.ranger.mtry.ratio = to_tune(0.1, 1),   regr.ranger.num.trees = to_tune(100, 500) ) at = auto_tuner(   tuner = tnr("random_search"),   learner = flrn,   resampling = rsmp("fcst.cv"),   measure = msr("regr.rmse"),   term_evals = 4 ) at$train(task) at$tuning_result[, .(regr.ranger.mtry.ratio, regr.ranger.num.trees, regr.rmse)] #>    regr.ranger.mtry.ratio regr.ranger.num.trees regr.rmse #> 1:              0.6814331                   235  25.22034  # the AutoTuner is itself a learner: predict with the best configuration at$predict(task, 142:144)$score(msr("regr.rmse")) #> regr.rmse  #>   13.6386 flrn = lrn("fcst.auto_arima") flrn$param_set$set_values(stationary = to_tune(p_lgl()), seasonal = to_tune(p_lgl())) at = auto_tuner(   tuner = tnr("grid_search"),   learner = flrn,   resampling = rsmp("fcst.holdout", ratio = 0.8),   measure = msr("regr.rmse") ) at$train(task) at$tuning_result[, .(stationary, seasonal, regr.rmse)] #>    stationary seasonal regr.rmse #> 1:      FALSE     TRUE  35.08279

title: Extending mlr3 to Time Series Forecasting
text: machine learning forecasting difference forecasting single time series longitudinal data often referred local global forecasting. global model trained jointly across many series, identified key:
code: library(mlr3learners) library(mlr3pipelines) library(tsibble)  dt = setDT(tsibbledata::aus_livestock) setnames(dt, tolower) dt[, month := as.Date(month)] dt = dt[, .(count = sum(count)), by = .(state, month)] setorder(dt, state, month) task = as_task_fcst(dt, id = "aus_livestock", target = "count", order = "month", key = "state", freq = "month") task$set_col_roles("month", add = "feature")  graph = po("fcst.lags", lags = 1:12) %>>%   po(     "datefeatures",     param_vals = list(       week_of_year = FALSE,       day_of_week = FALSE,       day_of_month = FALSE,       day_of_year = FALSE     )   ) %>>%   lrn("regr.ranger")  flrn = recursive_forecaster(graph)$train(task) prediction = flrn$predict(task, 4460:4464) prediction$score(msr("regr.rmse")) #> regr.rmse  #>  18500.26  resampling = rsmp("fcst.holdout", ratio = 0.9) rr = resample(task, flrn, resampling) rr$aggregate(msr("regr.rmse")) #> regr.rmse  #>  107559.2

title: Extending mlr3 to Time Series Forecasting
text: single global model can compared fitting one local model per series:
code: retail = setDT(tsibbledata::aus_retail) setnames(retail, tolower) retail[, month := as.Date(month)] vic = retail[state == "Victoria"] vic[, let(state = NULL, `series id` = NULL)] vic[, industry := as.factor(industry)] vic_train = vic[month < as.Date("2015-01-01")] vic_test = vic[month >= as.Date("2015-01-01")]  # global forecasting task_train = as_task_fcst(   vic_train,   id = "aus_retail_vic",   target = "turnover",   order = "month",   key = "industry",   freq = "month" ) task_test = as_task_fcst(   vic_test,   id = "aus_retail_vic",   target = "turnover",   order = "month",   key = "industry",   freq = "month" )  learner = lrn("regr.ranger", verbose = FALSE) flrn = recursive_forecaster(learner, lags = 1:12)$train(task_train) prediction_global = flrn$predict(task_test) prediction_global #>  #> ── <PredictionRegr> for 960 observations: ────────────────────────────────────── #>  row_ids truth response                                 industry      month #>        1 476.2 470.2078 Cafes, restaurants and catering services 2015-01-01 #>        2 422.0 455.6845 Cafes, restaurants and catering services 2015-02-01 #>        3 471.2 484.1151 Cafes, restaurants and catering services 2015-03-01 #>      ---   ---      ---                                      ---        --- #>      958 359.2 399.3639                   Takeaway food services 2018-10-01 #>      959 354.9 405.4836                   Takeaway food services 2018-11-01 #>      960 393.2 412.3130                   Takeaway food services 2018-12-01 prediction_global$score(msr("regr.rmse")) #> regr.rmse  #>  83.93126  # local forecasting prediction_local = map(split(vic, by = "industry", drop = TRUE), function(dt) {   task_train = as_task_fcst(     dt[month < as.Date("2015-01-01")],     id = "aus_retail_vic_local",     target = "turnover",     order = "month",     freq = "month"   )   task_test = as_task_fcst(     dt[month >= as.Date("2015-01-01")],     id = "aus_retail_vic_local",     target = "turnover",     order = "month",     freq = "month"   )   flrn = recursive_forecaster(learner, lags = 1:12)$train(task_train)   prediction = flrn$predict(task_test)   prediction }) do.call(c, prediction_local)$score(msr("regr.rmse")) #> regr.rmse  #>  95.02372

title: Direct Multi-Step Forecast Learner — DirectForecaster
text: Trains separate model forecast horizon. horizon h base lags 1:p, model h uses lags h:(h+p-1), prediction time observed values needed. Unlike RecursiveForecaster, predictions feed back subsequent steps (error accumulation). Lag features managed internally horizon-shifted offsets via lags – include iterative feature PipeOp (property "fcst_iterative", e.g. PipeOpFcstLags, PipeOpFcstRolling) learner graph. ops yet horizon-offset leak future information horizons > 1, rejected construction.

title: Direct Multi-Step Forecast Learner — DirectForecaster
text: learner (mlr3::Learner | mlr3pipelines::Graph | mlr3pipelines::PipeOp) regression learner graph/PipeOp (without PipeOpFcstLags). lags (integer()) base lag values. Exposed $param_set lags, can tuned via mlr3tuning::AutoTuner. horizons (integer()) Either single integer H (expanded 1:H) integer vector specific horizons. One model trained per horizon. predict time test row routed model matching step-distance end training, specific horizons (e.g. c(2L, 4L, 6L)) test set may contain rows exact steps ahead. id (character(1) | NULL) Identifier, default NULL (auto-generated learner id). param_vals (named list()) Hyperparameter values applied every horizon model. Per-horizon hyperparameters currently supported. predict_type (character(1) | NULL) predict type, default NULL.

code: library(mlr3pipelines)  task = tsk("airpassengers") split = partition(task, ratio = 0.8)  # simple: one model per horizon flrn = DirectForecaster$new(lrn("regr.rpart"), lags = 1:3, horizons = length(split$test)) flrn$train(task, split$train) flrn$predict(task, split$test) #>  #> ── <PredictionRegr> for 29 observations: ─────────────────────────────────────── #>  row_ids truth response      month #>      116   505 391.9375 1958-08-01 #>      117   404 324.7500 1958-09-01 #>      118   359 306.0000 1958-10-01 #>      ---   ---      ---        --- #>      142   461 368.6875 1960-10-01 #>      143   390 364.9333 1960-11-01 #>      144   432 362.3571 1960-12-01  # or use the direct_forecaster() helper flrn = direct_forecaster(lrn("regr.rpart"), lags = 1:3, horizons = length(split$test)) flrn$train(task, split$train) flrn$predict(task, split$test) #>  #> ── <PredictionRegr> for 29 observations: ─────────────────────────────────────── #>  row_ids truth response      month #>      116   505 391.9375 1958-08-01 #>      117   404 324.7500 1958-09-01 #>      118   359 306.0000 1958-10-01 #>      ---   ---      ---        --- #>      142   461 368.6875 1960-10-01 #>      143   390 364.9333 1960-11-01 #>      144   432 362.3571 1960-12-01

title: Forecast Learner — LearnerFcst
text: Learner specializes mlr3::LearnerRegr forecast problems: task_type set "fcst". Creates mlr3::Predictions class mlr3::PredictionRegr. Possible values predict_types : "response": Predicts numeric response observation test set. "se": Predicts standard error value response observation test set. "distr": Probability distribution VectorDistribution object (requires package distr6, available via repository https://raphaels1.r-universe.dev). Predefined learners can found dictionary mlr3::mlr_learners.

title: Recursive Forecast Learner — RecursiveForecaster
text: mlr3::Learner iterative one-step-ahead forecasting. Wraps mlr3pipelines::GraphLearner (held internally, mirroring DirectForecaster mlr3tuning::AutoTuner). Training fully delegated graph. predict time forecaster builds combined task (training history + test rows NA targets) backed single mutable data.table::data.table(), iterates test rows (key, order) order, writes prediction back combined task's target column lag rolling features next step reflect freshly predicted value. Can constructed two ways: Simple: RecursiveForecaster$new(learner, lags = 1:3) – internally builds po("fcst.lags", lags = lags) %>>% learner. Graph: RecursiveForecaster$new(graph) – takes arbitrary mlr3pipelines::Graph mlr3pipelines::PipeOp.

title: Recursive Forecast Learner — RecursiveForecaster
text: target transformation (e.g. mlr_pipeops_fcst.targetdiff, mlr3pipelines::PipeOpTargetMutate) must wrap forecaster, placed inside graph. Wrap mlr3pipelines::ppl("targettrafo") whole series transformed front, recursion runs entirely transformed scale, predictions inverted end:   Placing mlr3pipelines::PipeOpTargetTrafo inside graph supported rejected construction: entangle transformation iterative lag/rolling feedback, read original-scale backend, producing train/predict scale mismatch.
code: flrn = as_learner(ppl("targettrafo",   graph = RecursiveForecaster$new(lrn("regr.rpart"), lags = 1:12),   trafo_pipeop = po("fcst.targetdiff", lag = 1L) )) flrn$train(task, split$train) flrn$predict(task, split$test)  # predictions are on the original scale

Target lags rolling windows (PipeOpFcstLags, PipeOpFcstRolling) computed within series, future forecast grid built per series. Key columns also features default, usually want. Keys region product meaningful categorical covariates, exposing lets global model specialize per series. high-cardinality key, arbitrary series id many levels, passing raw factor learner can overfit fail learners need encoding. Encode inside learner graph instead, e.g. mlr3pipelines::PipeOpEncodeImpact (po("encodeimpact")) shrinkage encoder po("encodelmer"). compose RecursiveForecaster DirectForecaster graphs. title: Bagged Model Forecast Learner — mlr_learners_fcst.bagged
text: Bootstrap-aggregated forecasts. series resampled via Box-Cox/Loess moving block bootstrap Bergmeir, Hyndman, Benítez fn fit replicate; forecast averages across ensemble. Calls forecast::baggedModel() package forecast. fn model-fitting function applied bootstrap replicate (defaults forecast::ets()). function returning object compatible forecast::forecast() may passed, e.g. forecast::auto.arima() forecast::Arima() fixed orders via wrapper. number bootstrap replicates controlled num, block_size configures moving block length forecast::bld.mbb.bootstrap(). Prediction intervals forecast::forecast.baggedModel() empirical bootstrap range (configurable level), "response" offered predict_type.

Prediction performed via forecast::forecast.StructTS() yields point forecasts predictive intervals Kalman filter.

title: Empirical Coverage — mlr_measures_fcst.coverage

title: Mean Absolute Scaled Error — mlr_measures_fcst.mase
text: Measures mean absolute error forecast scaled -sample mean absolute error naive (seasonal naive) forecast. Values less one indicate forecast better naive baseline.

title: Mean Absolute Scaled Error — mlr_measures_fcst.mase
text: $$   \mathrm{MASE} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{=1}^n     \frac{\lvert y_i - \hat y_i \rvert}     {\frac{1}{T-m} \sum_{t=m+1}^T \lvert z_t - z_{t-m} \rvert} $$ \(z\) training series, \(m\) seasonal period, \(T\) length training series.

title: Mean Scaled Interval Score — mlr_measures_fcst.msis
text: Measures quality central prediction intervals, scaling interval (Winkler) score -sample mean absolute error naive (seasonal naive) forecast. interval score rewards narrow intervals penalizes observations falling outside , scaling makes measure comparable across series different magnitudes. prediction-interval metric used M4 competition. Smaller scores indicate better calibrated narrower intervals.

title: Mean Scaled Interval Score — mlr_measures_fcst.msis
text: central interval level 1 - alpha lower upper bounds \(l_i\) \(u_i\) (alpha/2 1 - alpha/2 quantiles): $$   \mathrm{MSIS} = \frac{\frac{1}{n} \sum_{=1}^n (u_i - l_i)     + \frac{2}{\alpha}(l_i - y_i)\mathbf{1}\{y_i < l_i\}     + \frac{2}{\alpha}(y_i - u_i)\mathbf{1}\{y_i > u_i\}}     {\frac{1}{T-m} \sum_{t=m+1}^T \lvert z_t - z_{t-m} \rvert} $$ \(z\) training series, \(m\) seasonal period, \(T\) length training series. keyed tasks score computed per series averaged.

title: Pinball Loss — mlr_measures_fcst.pinball
text: Measures quality quantile (probabilistic) forecasts using pinball loss, also known quantile loss. loss averaged observations predicted quantile levels. Smaller scores indicate better calibrated quantile forecasts.

title: Pinball Loss — mlr_measures_fcst.pinball
text: single quantile level \(\tau\) forecast \(q_i\) observation \(y_i\) pinball loss $$   L_\tau(y_i, q_i) =   \begin{cases}     \tau\,(y_i - q_i), & \text{} y_i \ge q_i \\     (1 - \tau)\,(q_i - y_i), & \text{} y_i < q_i   \end{cases} $$ reported score twice mean \(L_\tau\) observations quantile levels \(\tau\), matching convention used fabletools median (\(\tau = 0.5\)) pinball loss equals mean absolute error.

Winkler score mean \(W_i\) observations.

title: Create Rolling Window Features of Target Variable — mlr_pipeops_fcst.rolling
text: Creates rolling-window summary statistics target variable new feature columns. window ends position t - lag (exclusive current lag - 1 recent values) size window_size. Use window_size = Inf expanding window grows include history t - lag. train time rows whose window insufficient history NA dropped, matching PipeOpFcstLags. Predict keeps rows. predict time, rolling features computed task's full backend (.e. including rows outside row_roles$use), joined onto active rows. Used inside RecursiveForecaster, forecaster writes step's prediction combined task's target column steps rolling features next step reflect freshly predicted value.

Default "mean". window_sizes :: numeric() Window sizes. Every combination funs window_sizes produces one output column. Finite sizes must whole numbers; Inf requests expanding window (history t - lag). Default 3L. lag :: integer(1) Minimum lag window starts. Must >= 1 avoid leakage. Default 1L.

title: Box-Cox Transform the Target Variable — mlr_pipeops_fcst.targetboxcox
text: Applies Box-Cox transformation target variable stabilize variance, producing new target BoxCox(y, lambda). transformation pointwise monotonic, rows dropped predictions inverted via forecast::InvBoxCox(). lambda = 0 log transformation. lambda NULL (default) estimated training data via forecast::BoxCox.lambda(), using task frequency "guerrero" method seasonality accounted . estimated (supplied) lambda stored reused predict time inversion. Box-Cox log transformations require strictly positive target values; non-positive values produce NaN error forecast::BoxCox().

See forecast::BoxCox.lambda(). lower :: numeric(1) Lower bound estimated lambda. Default -1. upper :: numeric(1) Upper bound estimated lambda. Default 2.

title: Box-Cox Transform the Target Variable — mlr_pipeops_fcst.targetboxcox

title: Difference the Target Variable — mlr_pipeops_fcst.targetdiff
text: Differences target variable lag lag, producing new target y'_t = y_t - y_{t - lag}. first lag rows dropped training. Predictions inverted via stride-lag cumulative sums anchored last lag training values, yielding original-scale predictions. Use lag = 1 remove trend lag = 12 (seasonal period) remove seasonality.

title: Difference the Target Variable — mlr_pipeops_fcst.targetdiff
text: parameters parameters inherited mlr3pipelines::PipeOpTargetTrafo, well following: lag :: integer(1) Lag difference . Default 1L.

title: Difference the Target Variable — mlr_pipeops_fcst.targetdiff
text: PipeOp must placed inside RecursiveForecaster DirectForecaster graph rejected construction. Inside RecursiveForecaster, trafo transforms active row predict time iterative features (lags, rolling windows) need transformed values historical rows. Inside DirectForecaster, horizon inverted independently training tail, wrong horizons >= 2. Use inside plain mlr3pipelines::GraphLearner via ppl("targettrafo", ...) batch prediction, wrap forecaster ppl("targettrafo", ...) horizons inverted together.

what: A. No real probabilistic forecasting for the ML (reduction) side
title: mlr3forecast — Package Review
text: , view, single important gap. Modern forecasting (M5/M6, industry demand planning) largely probabilistic, package’s measure set leans way (Pinball, Winkler, Coverage, MSIS). Yet: Recursive multi-step prediction feeds point $response back feature matrix step (R/RecursiveForecaster.R, predict loop ~L119-124: set(combined, ..., value = prediction$response)). simulation / bootstrap path, multi-step predictive distributions propagated — quantiles ’d get conditional point estimates earlier steps systematically -dispersed. Direct principle emit per-horizon quantiles base regr learner , calibrated-interval machinery around . Classical wrappers get intervals “free” upstream packages, hides asymmetry: headline ML story effectively point-forecast . deserves () explicit documentation limitation (b) roadmap item simulation/bootstrap-based predictive distribution.

what: B. The “forecasting = regr + an order column” abstraction is leaky, and the leaks are runtime-enforced
title: mlr3forecast — Package Review
text: reuse story package’s biggest strength, abstraction several sharp, non-obvious exceptions surface runtime errors: Classical LearnerFcst* learners reject keyed (global) tasks (R/LearnerFcst.R:79-81, “support tasks keys”). “global forecasting” ML-reduction-capability; classical models must hand-roll split/loop (exactly README’s “local forecasting” example ). asymmetry signposted. Target-transform pipeops must placed inside Recursive/Direct graph (man/mlr_pipeops_fcst.targetboxcox.Rd, Limitations section). -sample future rows mixed one predict() (R/LearnerFcst.R:104-108). “Forecasters behave like mlr3 learner” pitch, enough composition rules capability/property advertisement (least consolidated “compose” doc) warranted. Otherwise users discover boundaries hitting errors.

what: C. Evaluation / backtesting is thin for a forecasting package
title: mlr3forecast — Package Review
text: two resamplings exist (fcst.holdout, fcst.cv). Missing, relative serious forecasting work expects: First-class rolling-origin evaluation per-horizon error breakdown (error--h standard diagnostic; get aggregate). hierarchical / grouped reconciliation (fable’s core strength). Global models exist via keys, coherent aggregation across hierarchy . fair scope choice early package, stated explicitly, ’s main axis users compare fable/fpp3.

what: D. Breadth-over-depth bet: 34 learners across ~10 optional backends
title: mlr3forecast — Package Review
text: package wraps wide roster (forecast, smooth, prophet, tscount, nnfor, Rlgt, Rcatch22, feasts, tsfeatures, …; Suggests). adapter thin, collectively large surface keep green upstreams drift, backends heavy (prophet pulls Stan toolchain). Worth deliberate decision: goal breadth (wrap everything, accept maintenance/testing matrix) clean core + exemplars? Right now ’s breadth, real long-term commitment — per-learner tests (~50 test_fcst_*.R files) confirm cost already paid.
